briefing.headline: 11 high-priority items in the latest EM scan
briefing.summary: Top sources today: European Journal of Emergency Medicine, MedPage Today Emergency Medicine, REBEL EM. Main themes: trials, point-of-care ultrasound, guideline or formal recommendation. Best early reads: Association between patient-to-nurse ratios in emergency departments and patient, staff, and organizational outcomes: a systematic review; Whole Blood Not Better Than Components En Route to Hospital After Traumatic Injury.
briefing.editorialHeadline: Targeting the Source: Advances in PE and Trauma Resuscitation
briefing.editorialSummary:
The evidence base continues to refine our approach to high-acuity, multi-system failure. For intermediate-risk pulmonary embolism, the HI-PEITHO trial suggests that while anticoagulation remains foundational, adding ultrasound-guided, catheter-directed fibrinolysis may offer a tangible benefit in preventing short-term hemodynamic collapse in the sickest patients. Similarly, in the trauma bay, the TOWAR trial provides reassuring data suggesting that administering whole, type O blood en route to the hospital is a clinically equivalent and potentially simpler alternative to complex component therapy for managing anticipated hemorrhagic shock.

Beyond the pulmonary and hemorrhagic pathways, procedural efficiency is gaining traction. For routine vascular access in young children, incorporating a liquid crystal vein locator appears to yield measurable improvements in first-attempt success rates. Furthermore, for acute rib fractures, the data supporting the addition of a Serratus Anterior Plane Block to standard analgesia suggests a valuable, readily implementable adjunct for improving pain control scores in the ED setting. These findings, alongside the continued refinement of transfusion triggers in high-risk cardiac patients, underscore a trend toward targeted, procedural, and resource-aware interventions.

This collection demands attention because it moves beyond broad guidelines, offering specific, actionable algorithms—from localized thrombolysis to improved vascular access—that can immediately refine care at the bedside.

dailyEditorial.title: Association between patient-to-nurse ratios in emergency departments and patient, staff, and organizational outcomes: a systematic review
dailyEditorial.takeaway: While the concept of setting nurse ratios sounds appealing for quality improvement, remember that the direct link between established ratios and reduced ED mortality remains unproven based on this preliminary overview. Don't change staffing models based on the premise alone; wait for the full results to see if the evidence supports a direct impact on patient death rates. Be cautious about generalizing findings from other care settings to our chaotic ED environment.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: This systematic review tackles the perennial question of whether implementing formal patient-to-nurse ratios in the emergency department translates to improved patient outcomes, specifically mortality.
dailyEditorial.summary: This systematic review tackles the perennial question of whether implementing formal patient-to-nurse ratios in the emergency department translates to improved patient outcomes, specifically mortality. The authors synthesized literature published between 2004 and 2025, which is a broad scope for such an intervention. While the general literature suggests that establishing nurse ratios benefits both patients and staff in various care settings, the applicability of these findings to the unique, high-acuity environment of the ED remains uncertain. The primary goal of this review was to assess the direct association between these ratios and ED mortality. However, the provided excerpt only details the methodology—the search strategy across major databases and the independent review process—without presenting the actual findings regarding mortality rates.

dailyEditorial.title: Whole Blood Not Better Than Components En Route to Hospital After Traumatic Injury
dailyEditorial.takeaway: When managing trauma patients with anticipated hemorrhagic shock, whole, type O blood appears to be a viable resuscitation option equivalent to component therapy en route. This might simplify prehospital blood product management. However, always consider the specific institutional protocols and the patient's ongoing clinical picture, as this study only compares the two approaches.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: The TOWAR randomized trial provides data suggesting that for trauma patients at risk of hemorrhagic shock, administering whole, type O blood en route to the hospital is comparably effective to administering pre-transfus…
dailyEditorial.summary: The TOWAR randomized trial provides data suggesting that for trauma patients at risk of hemorrhagic shock, administering whole, type O blood en route to the hospital is comparably effective to administering pre-transfusion blood components. The study's primary finding is that the clinical outcomes were similar between the two transfusion strategies. This suggests that the logistical simplicity and availability of whole blood might make it an acceptable alternative to the more complex component-based resuscitation protocols in the prehospital or immediate pre-hospital setting. While the results are encouraging for resource management, it's important to remember that this compares two established practices rather than establishing a new standard of care.

dailyEditorial.title: HI-PEITHO Trial: Ultrasound-facilitated, Catheter Directed Fibrinolysis for PE
dailyEditorial.takeaway: For intermediate-risk PE, standard anticoagulation remains the bedrock of management. Consider US-CDT plus anticoagulation primarily for those patients exhibiting signs of significant hemodynamic instability, as this combination showed benefit in preventing short-term decompensation in that subgroup. Remember that this advanced therapy is not universally indicated for all intermediate-risk patients.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: The HI-PEITHO trial was a randomized controlled trial designed to test whether adding ultrasound-facilitated, catheter-directed fibrinolysis (US-CDT) to standard anticoagulation improves outcomes for intermediate-risk p…
dailyEditorial.summary: The HI-PEITHO trial was a randomized controlled trial designed to test whether adding ultrasound-facilitated, catheter-directed fibrinolysis (US-CDT) to standard anticoagulation improves outcomes for intermediate-risk pulmonary embolism (PE). The primary question addressed was whether this combination therapy offers an advantage over anticoagulation alone in this specific patient cohort. The key takeaway from the analysis is that the combination approach demonstrated a benefit specifically in mitigating short-term hemodynamic decompensation among the sickest subset of intermediate-risk PE patients. Overall, the results suggest a potential role for this advanced intervention in the most unstable intermediate-risk patients, while still emphasizing the foundational role of anticoagulation.

dailyEditorial.title: Effect of a liquid crystal vein locator on intravenous catheterization in young children: a randomized trial
dailyEditorial.takeaway: For routine PIVC in young children, incorporating a liquid crystal vein locator appears beneficial, as it is associated with better first-pass success and fewer attempts. This could streamline care and reduce iatrogenic distress during emergent procedures. However, remember that this is based on a single trial, and its routine adoption should be weighed against the added cost and time of the device itself.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: This randomized trial assessed the utility of a liquid crystal vein locator during peripheral intravenous catheterization (PIVC) specifically in the pediatric population.
dailyEditorial.summary: This randomized trial assessed the utility of a liquid crystal vein locator during peripheral intravenous catheterization (PIVC) specifically in the pediatric population. The investigators compared the use of this locator against standard catheterization techniques. The key finding reported was that employing the liquid crystal vein locator correlated with several positive outcomes: a significantly higher first-attempt success rate, a reduction in the total number of puncture attempts, and shorter overall procedural times. These results suggest a tangible procedural benefit when using this technology for accessing veins in young children. Overall, the data points toward an improved efficiency and potentially less traumatic experience during necessary vascular access procedures in this age group.

dailyEditorial.title: TOP: Liberal or restrictive post-op transfusion
dailyEditorial.takeaway: For high-risk cardiac patients with postoperative anemia, the data from TOP support maintaining a more restrictive transfusion strategy, favoring an Hb threshold closer to 7 g/dL over a liberal approach. Continue to weigh the risks of transfusion against the benefits of maintaining hemoglobin levels, remembering that the primary goal is preventing major adverse cardiac events. Be mindful that this trial focused on major vascular or general surgery, and extrapolation to all surgical types should be done cautiously.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: The TOP trial directly addressed the transfusion strategy for postoperative anemia in high-risk cardiac patients, comparing a liberal threshold (Hb <10 g/dL) against a restrictive one (Hb <7 g/dL).
dailyEditorial.summary: The TOP trial directly addressed the transfusion strategy for postoperative anemia in high-risk cardiac patients, comparing a liberal threshold (Hb <10 g/dL) against a restrictive one (Hb <7 g/dL). The primary endpoint was the reduction of 90-day all-cause death or major ischemic events. Given the known cardiac comorbidities in this group, the decision on transfusion trigger is critical for balancing oxygen delivery against potential transfusion-related risks. While the current literature leans toward more conservative transfusion practices, this trial provides a direct comparison in a high-risk surgical cohort. The results are important for solidifying evidence-based guidelines in this challenging clinical scenario.

dailyEditorial.title: Serratus Anterior Plane Block for Acute Rib Fractures in the Emergency Department: A Randomized Controlled Trial
dailyEditorial.takeaway: For acute rib fractures in the ED, consider adding an SAPB to standard analgesia, as this approach was shown to significantly improve pain control scores. Since the procedure was reported as safe and feasible for ED physicians, it represents a valuable, readily implementable adjunct. Remember that while the data is positive, this is based on a single trial, so careful consideration of local resources and institutional protocols is warranted.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: This randomized controlled trial evaluated the utility of adding a Serratus Anterior Plane Block (SAPB) to standard analgesic management for acute rib fractures encountered in the emergency department.
dailyEditorial.summary: This randomized controlled trial evaluated the utility of adding a Serratus Anterior Plane Block (SAPB) to standard analgesic management for acute rib fractures encountered in the emergency department. The primary finding is that incorporating the SAPB significantly improved Pain Intensity Control (PIC) scores in this patient population. The authors emphasize that the procedure was performed safely by emergency physicians, suggesting it is a practical and scalable adjunct. Overall, the data supports the integration of this regional anesthetic technique into the ED armamentarium for managing rib fracture pain.

dailyEditorial.title: 2025 guideline updates for community-acquired pneumonia diagnosis and management
dailyEditorial.takeaway: For stable, nonsevere CAP, remember the updated preference for shorter antibiotic courses, aiming for a minimum of three days rather than longer regimens. Don't hesitate to leverage lung ultrasound if you have experienced personnel available, as this is now supported. However, be mindful that the recommendation for withholding antibiotics in viral-positive, otherwise healthy outpatients is a significant shift that requires careful risk stratification.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: The new 2025 American Thoracic Society guidelines provide an updated framework for diagnosing and managing community-acquired pneumonia (CAP) encountered in the emergency department.
dailyEditorial.summary: The new 2025 American Thoracic Society guidelines provide an updated framework for diagnosing and managing community-acquired pneumonia (CAP) encountered in the emergency department. These guidelines touch upon imaging modalities, antibiotic stewardship, and the role of corticosteroids. A notable shift is the increased support for utilizing lung ultrasound by experienced clinicians in the ED setting. Furthermore, the recommendations suggest a more nuanced approach to antibiotic initiation, particularly for otherwise healthy outpatients who test positive for a virus. Clinically, the guidelines favor shorter antibiotic courses, recommending a minimum of three days for both stable outpatients and admitted patients with nonsevere CAP.

dailyEditorial.title: Catheter-Directed Fibrinolysis for Pulmonary Embolism: Is It Old NEWS?
dailyEditorial.takeaway: Consider ultrasound-guided, catheter-directed fibrinolysis as a targeted option for acute PE, especially in patients with contraindications or high bleeding risk to systemic thrombolytics. While promising, remember that this is a specialized procedure requiring appropriate imaging and procedural expertise. Always assess the patient's bleeding risk profile before considering this intervention.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: This article details the use of ultrasound-guided, catheter-directed fibrinolysis as a treatment strategy for acute pulmonary embolism.
dailyEditorial.summary: This article details the use of ultrasound-guided, catheter-directed fibrinolysis as a treatment strategy for acute pulmonary embolism. The authors present data supporting this minimally invasive approach, which aims to directly administer thrombolytic agents to the pulmonary embolus site. The technique utilizes ultrasound guidance to precisely place the catheter, allowing for targeted fibrinolysis. This represents a shift toward more localized and potentially safer reperfusion strategies compared to systemic thrombolysis. The findings suggest that this method offers a viable alternative for patients with acute PE, particularly those who might be at high risk for bleeding from systemic agents.

dailyEditorial.title: Modified brain injury guidelines in a regional Australian setting indicate significant resource savings but also safety concerns
dailyEditorial.takeaway: When considering TBI management pathways, remember that while guidelines like mBIG can significantly curb unnecessary resource use, their validation is context-specific. Do not assume US-based protocols translate directly; local evaluation is necessary to balance resource savings against potential safety gaps. Always be mindful of the local standard of care regarding imaging and consultation thresholds.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: Traumatic brain injury remains a significant burden in the emergency department, with current practices often leading to substantial resource utilization, including unnecessary neurosurgical consultations and repeat ima…
dailyEditorial.summary: Traumatic brain injury remains a significant burden in the emergency department, with current practices often leading to substantial resource utilization, including unnecessary neurosurgical consultations and repeat imaging, even when the majority of injuries are mild. The modified Brain Injury Guidelines (mBIG), which originated in the US, offer a structured approach to stratify TBI severity with the goal of safely reducing these resource drains. While these US-developed pathways have shown promise in improving resource use without compromising patient outcomes, their applicability and safety profile within the specific context of Australian trauma care have not been previously assessed. This local evaluation is therefore important for determining if adopting such a guideline modification is appropriate for our regional setting.

dailyEditorial.title: Evaluating the TWIST score and point-of-care ultrasound for paediatric testicular torsion
dailyEditorial.takeaway: When evaluating acute scrotal pain in a child, remember that while the TWIST score and POCUS are highly sensitive and specific tools when performed by ED staff, the residual false-negative rate means you cannot rely on a negative score to completely rule out torsion. Always maintain a high index of suspicion, especially in equivocal cases, and consider proceeding with further imaging or urology consultation if clinical suspicion remains elevated despite a reassuring score.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: This study assessed the diagnostic utility of the TWIST score combined with point-of-care ultrasound (POCUS) for testicular torsion in children up to 15 years old, specifically when interpreted by emergency physicians o…
dailyEditorial.summary: This study assessed the diagnostic utility of the TWIST score combined with point-of-care ultrasound (POCUS) for testicular torsion in children up to 15 years old, specifically when interpreted by emergency physicians or pediatricians. The overall findings suggest that both the TWIST score and POCUS performed by non-urology specialists maintain high diagnostic accuracy for this critical condition. The TWIST score demonstrated a high sensitivity of 91% and a specificity of 95% against the final diagnosis. However, the authors caution that the non-negligible false-negative rate means the score should be utilized for risk stratification rather than serving as a definitive exclusionary tool. Furthermore, when defining positivity based on impaired blood flow or the whirlpool sign, the sensitivity and specificity remained high, though the positive predictive value was lower.

dailyEditorial.title: Journal update monthly top five
dailyEditorial.takeaway: When managing septic shock, remember that while mortality remains high, the literature is actively searching for actionable predictors. Pay close attention to the specific risk factors identified in the Jung et al. meta-analysis, as this represents a 'head turner' concept. However, treat these findings as suggestive rather than definitive, as the authors caution about the need for further validation before changing established resuscitation bundles.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: This month's editorial update synthesizes five high-yield papers from outside the core field of emergency medicine, curated by residents in South West England using a multimodal search approach.
dailyEditorial.summary: This month's editorial update synthesizes five high-yield papers from outside the core field of emergency medicine, curated by residents in South West England using a multimodal search approach. A significant focus area is the persistent challenge of identifying prognostic factors for mortality in septic shock, where the authors highlight a systematic review and meta-analysis by Jung et al. This review specifically addresses the current gap in clear risk factor stratification for high mortality in this critical condition. The authors categorize the presented literature, ranging from 'Worth a peek' to 'Game changer,' guiding the reader on immediate clinical utility. The emphasis on synthesizing external evidence suggests these findings could refine current resuscitation protocols.

dailyEditorial.title: Point-of-care ultrasound by trained emergency physicians versus radiologists for deep vein thrombosis diagnosis: a prospective blinded study on diagnostic accuracy and time efficiency
dailyEditorial.takeaway: Given the high sensitivity and specificity achieved by trained ED physicians, you can feel confident utilizing bedside compression ultrasound for suspected DVT. This approach offers a significant time advantage over traditional radiology reads. Remember that while the diagnostic accuracy is excellent, the study suggests future research must prove that this faster diagnosis actually translates into improved patient flow or shorter ED stays.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: This prospective, blinded study compared the diagnostic performance and efficiency of deep vein thrombosis (DVT) ultrasound performed by trained emergency physicians versus the established standard of care using radiolo…
dailyEditorial.summary: This prospective, blinded study compared the diagnostic performance and efficiency of deep vein thrombosis (DVT) ultrasound performed by trained emergency physicians versus the established standard of care using radiologist-performed Doppler ultrasound. The results were quite striking, showing that emergency physicians achieved high diagnostic accuracy, reporting a sensitivity of 97.0% and a specificity of 99.3% for suspected lower-extremity DVT. Furthermore, the time savings were substantial, with the median time-to-diagnosis being one hour for the ED physician versus four hours for the radiologist. The excellent interobserver agreement, with kappa values generally above 0.90, strongly supports the utility of point-of-care ultrasound in this setting. Ultimately, the authors suggest this supports earlier bedside diagnosis, though they caution that further work is needed to link this efficiency gain to tangible clinical outcomes like reduced length of stay.

dailyEditorial.title: Design Refinements for Sepsis Albumin Trials: Building on ICARUS-ED Experience
dailyEditorial.takeaway: The feasibility demonstrated in ICARUS-ED suggests that incorporating ward-based patients into sepsis trials is achievable, which broadens the scope of evidence for early resuscitation. While the albumin intervention itself is the focus of the trial, the key takeaway for practice is the operational blueprint for conducting these complex, real-time studies. Remember that the high rate of confirmed infection in the cohort is encouraging, but the results should guide trial design rather than immediate, routine changes to resuscitation protocols.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: This piece reviews the ICARUS-ED pilot randomized controlled trial, which explored the use of concentrated albumin for undifferentiated sepsis management directly within the emergency department setting.
dailyEditorial.summary: This piece reviews the ICARUS-ED pilot randomized controlled trial, which explored the use of concentrated albumin for undifferentiated sepsis management directly within the emergency department setting. The authors highlight that the trial successfully demonstrated the logistical feasibility of conducting real-time sepsis research, which is a significant hurdle in this area. Notably, the study managed to enroll and maintain high protocol compliance, even incorporating ward-based patients alongside those destined for the ICU. Furthermore, they reported confirming an infection in a high percentage of the enrolled cohort, despite the inherent diagnostic ambiguity often present in early sepsis presentations. Overall, the work provides valuable operational data for future, larger-scale trials in sepsis resuscitation.

dailyEditorial.title: Systematic Review of Pediatric Ketamine in Emergency Department Procedural Sedation: Frequency and Predictors of Adverse Events
dailyEditorial.takeaway: When considering ketamine for procedural sedation in pediatrics, remember that this review attempts to standardize the reporting of adverse events and predictors across multiple studies. While it provides a good overview, the clinical utility of the identified predictors might still be subject to variability across different ED protocols and patient cohorts. Approach these pooled estimates cautiously, recognizing that individual patient assessment remains paramount.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: This systematic review synthesizes existing literature to provide a comprehensive overview of adverse events associated with using intravenous ketamine for procedural sedation in the pediatric emergency department.
dailyEditorial.summary: This systematic review synthesizes existing literature to provide a comprehensive overview of adverse events associated with using intravenous ketamine for procedural sedation in the pediatric emergency department. The authors focused on pooling data from studies involving a substantial number of pediatric patients, specifically those with ≥250 subjects, to establish more robust frequency estimates for these complications. Beyond just listing adverse events, the review also takes the time to catalog and estimate the clinical significance of various predictor variables that have been previously identified in the literature. This synthesis is useful because it moves beyond anecdotal reports to give a more quantitative picture of the safety profile when using ketamine for sedation in this vulnerable population.

dailyEditorial.title: Methodological limitations of NOM in the WSES acute appendicitis guidelines
dailyEditorial.takeaway: When considering NOM for acute appendicitis, remember that the diagnostic yield of admission CT is imperfect, potentially missing microperforations or occult neoplasms. Given the substantial recurrence rate of 42.6%, these methodological limitations suggest caution, and the decision to proceed with NOM should weigh these diagnostic uncertainties heavily against the potential for recurrence.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: This piece dives into the methodological caveats surrounding the use of nonoperative management (NOM) for acute appendicitis, even though the 2025 WSES Guidelines currently support it for uncomplicated presentations.
dailyEditorial.summary: This piece dives into the methodological caveats surrounding the use of nonoperative management (NOM) for acute appendicitis, even though the 2025 WSES Guidelines currently support it for uncomplicated presentations. The authors apply the GRADE framework to critique the diagnostic utility of admission computed tomography, pointing out significant limitations. Specifically, the article highlights that CT can severely underestimate microperforations and has a documented failure rate of up to 3.2% in excluding occult appendiceal neoplasms. Given these diagnostic uncertainties and the reported recurrence rates reaching 42.6%, the authors urge a more critical look at the current guidelines supporting NOM.

dailyEditorial.title: Presepsin for sepsis diagnosis in emergency departments: a multicentre study
dailyEditorial.takeaway: While presepsin showed a slight edge over PCT in diagnostic accuracy for sepsis and septic shock, remember that its utility is supportive, not definitive. Do not let a single biomarker result dictate your resuscitation or antibiotic timing; always integrate these findings with the patient's clinical picture and lactate trends. These markers are best used to help stratify risk when the diagnosis remains unclear.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: This multicenter study assessed the utility of presepsin and procalcitonin (PCT) for diagnosing sepsis and septic shock in the emergency department, utilizing the Sepsis-3 criteria.
dailyEditorial.summary: This multicenter study assessed the utility of presepsin and procalcitonin (PCT) for diagnosing sepsis and septic shock in the emergency department, utilizing the Sepsis-3 criteria. The authors found that presepsin demonstrated slightly superior diagnostic accuracy compared to PCT for both sepsis and septic shock, and importantly, elevated levels correlated with increased 30-day mortality. While the diagnostic accuracy metrics, such as the AUROC, were modest overall, the data suggests a potential role for these biomarkers in early risk stratification. The key takeaway emphasized by the research is that these markers should function as adjuncts to, rather than replacements for, established clinical judgment and standard diagnostic workup.

dailyEditorial.title: Should anticoagulants be initiated in patients with sepsis-induced new-onset atrial fibrillation? Best evidence topic report
dailyEditorial.takeaway: For patients with sepsis-induced new-onset AF, do not initiate routine anticoagulation solely for stroke prevention based on current evidence. The observational data is unconvincing, showing no consistent stroke benefit and even suggesting increased risk in some cohorts. Proceed with caution, recognizing that a definitive recommendation requires a dedicated randomized controlled trial.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: This topic report synthesizes the current evidence regarding the use of anticoagulation for stroke prevention in patients presenting with new-onset atrial fibrillation (AF) secondary to sepsis.
dailyEditorial.summary: This topic report synthesizes the current evidence regarding the use of anticoagulation for stroke prevention in patients presenting with new-onset atrial fibrillation (AF) secondary to sepsis. The review analyzed four observational studies, and the findings were not conclusive for routine anticoagulation. Notably, none of the studies demonstrated a clear reduction in stroke risk associated with starting therapeutic anticoagulation in this setting, and one large observation even suggested a paradoxical increase in stroke incidence. While bleeding risk was not consistently elevated, one study did report a potential benefit in terms of reduced mortality with anticoagulation. Given these mixed and somewhat conflicting results, the authors emphasize that a randomized controlled trial is necessary to definitively guide practice.

dailyEditorial.title: Validation of The Resuscitation Management (THERM) score in the emergency department
dailyEditorial.takeaway: When managing critically ill patients in the ED, consider using the THERM score as a potentially superior adjunct to existing early warning systems. Remember the specific calculation—bicarbonate plus GCS, minus 4 if SBP is low—as this structured approach might improve risk stratification over relying solely on NEWS2. However, given this is a validation paper, its utility should be weighed against the established workflow and local institutional protocols.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: This paper presents the validation of the Resuscitation Management (THERM) score, a newly developed prediction tool intended for use with critically ill patients in the emergency department setting.
dailyEditorial.summary: This paper presents the validation of the Resuscitation Management (THERM) score, a newly developed prediction tool intended for use with critically ill patients in the emergency department setting. The score is calculated using a combination of readily available parameters: the bicarbonate level (capped at 22 mmol/L), the total Glasgow Coma Scale, and a deduction of 4 points if the systolic blood pressure is low. The authors report that in their initial validation, THERM demonstrated significantly superior performance compared to established tools like the National Early Warning Score (NEWS) and its updated iteration, NEWS2. This suggests THERM may offer a more robust, easily implementable risk stratification method for guiding resuscitation efforts in the acute care environment.

dailyEditorial.title: A Woman With Rapidly Progressive Weakness
dailyEditorial.takeaway: When you see acute, flaccid paralysis with a distinct sensory level in a patient with CKD, think beyond the obvious causes. The combination of profound weakness, areflexia, and a sensory deficit below the nipple-to-xiphoid level warrants an urgent workup for acute polyneuropathies. Don't miss metabolic derangements or toxin exposures, as the differential is broad.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: This case report details a 57-year-old woman with known chronic kidney disease presenting with acute, rapidly progressive, and nontraumatic lower extremity weakness and numbness.
dailyEditorial.summary: This case report details a 57-year-old woman with known chronic kidney disease presenting with acute, rapidly progressive, and nontraumatic lower extremity weakness and numbness. On examination, she exhibited profound flaccid paralysis in the legs, absent reflexes, and a distinct pattern of sensory loss affecting pain and temperature below the nipple-to-xiphoid level. The presentation is highly suggestive of an acute neuromuscular process, given the combination of motor and sensory deficits in a patient with underlying CKD. The authors highlight the need to consider a broad differential diagnosis when encountering such an acute, symmetric, and profound polyneuropathy in the emergency setting, especially when underlying systemic comorbidities are present.

dailyEditorial.title: Peak Expiratory flow Rate for Emergency Department Management of Acute Asthma Exacerbation: A Randomized Controlled Trial Pilot Study
dailyEditorial.takeaway: For acute asthma exacerbations in the ED, current evidence from this pilot trial suggests that using PEFR measurements to guide therapy over symptom assessment does not improve outcomes or resource use. Continue to manage patients based on a comprehensive clinical assessment, and be cautious about over-relying on PEFR alone to dictate the level of care provided. A definitive change in practice requires larger, more robust studies.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: This pilot randomized controlled trial compared the outcomes of using Peak Expiratory Flow Rate (PEFR) to guide care versus relying on symptom assessment for managing acute asthma exacerbations in the Emergency Departme…
dailyEditorial.summary: This pilot randomized controlled trial compared the outcomes of using Peak Expiratory Flow Rate (PEFR) to guide care versus relying on symptom assessment for managing acute asthma exacerbations in the Emergency Department. The study found that guiding treatment based on PEFR measurements did not result in any measurable improvement in symptom control, nor did it impact resource utilization or the final disposition of the patients compared to the standard symptom-guided approach. Overall, the data suggest that incorporating PEFR into the routine management algorithm for acute asthma in the ED may not offer a tangible benefit. Given these results, the authors emphasize that larger, multicenter trials are necessary before any practice changes can be considered.
